# Harness

An autonomous plan-build loop for Claude Code. Harness takes behavioral specs and autonomously researches, plans, implements, verifies, and commits — one phase at a time.

## What's Inside

```
harness/
├── loop.sh                    # Orchestrator — runs the plan/build state machine
├── parse_stream.py            # Stream parser — filters Claude output, detects signals
├── IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md     # State index — tracks active/staging/completed work
├── SPEC_core.md               # Spec template — language-agnostic service specification
├── SPEC_language.md           # Spec language guide — how to word specifications
├── SPEC_shell.md              # Spec example — filled-in spec for reference
├── .claude/
│   ├── settings.json          # Claude Code project settings
│   ├── commands/              # Slash commands (skills)
│   │   ├── ralph_plan.md      # Planning state machine (PLAN mode)
│   │   ├── ralph_build.md     # Build executor (BUILD mode)
│   │   ├── create_jtbd.md     # Jobs to Be Done document creation
│   │   ├── create_plan.md     # Implementation plan creation
│   │   ├── create_ralph_topics.md  # JTBD → spec decomposition
│   │   ├── implement_plan.md  # Phase-by-phase implementation
│   │   ├── iterate_plan.md    # Plan iteration/updates
│   │   ├── research_codebase.md        # Full codebase research
│   │   ├── research_codebase_lightweight.md  # Quick followup research
│   │   └── commit.md          # Git commit helper
│   └── agents/                # Subagent definitions
│       ├── codebase-analyzer.md       # HOW code works
│       ├── codebase-locator.md        # WHERE code lives
│       ├── codebase-pattern-finder.md # Existing patterns + examples
│       ├── open-questions-evaluator.md # Research question triage
│       ├── thoughts-analyzer.md       # Extract insights from docs
│       ├── thoughts-locator.md        # Find docs in thoughts/
│       └── web-search-researcher.md   # Web research
└── thoughts/shared/           # Durable knowledge store
    ├── jtbd/                  # Jobs to Be Done documents
    ├── specs/                 # Behavioral spec files
    ├── plans/                 # Implementation plans
    └── research/              # Research documents
```

## Quick Start

### 1. Copy harness into your project

```bash
# From your project root
git clone https://github.com/lightfastai/harness.git /tmp/harness-src

# Copy the harness files
cp /tmp/harness-src/loop.sh .
cp /tmp/harness-src/parse_stream.py .
cp /tmp/harness-src/IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md .
cp /tmp/harness-src/.gitignore .  # or merge with your existing one
cp -r /tmp/harness-src/.claude .
cp -r /tmp/harness-src/thoughts .

# Optional: copy spec templates
cp /tmp/harness-src/SPEC_core.md .
cp /tmp/harness-src/SPEC_language.md .

chmod +x loop.sh

rm -rf /tmp/harness-src
```

### 2. Write your SPEC.md

Create a `SPEC.md` in your project root. Use `SPEC_core.md` as a template and `SPEC_language.md` for style guidance.

### 3. Create a JTBD document

```
claude
> /create_jtbd
```

This interactively creates a Jobs to Be Done document that captures the strategic "why" behind your product.

### 4. Decompose into specs

```
> /create_ralph_topics thoughts/shared/jtbd/YYYY-MM-DD-description.md
```

This decomposes the JTBD into Topics of Concern — each becomes a behavioral spec file in `thoughts/shared/specs/`.

### 5. Seed the implementation plan

After specs are created, populate `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md`:

```markdown
# Implementation Plan

Source: thoughts/shared/specs/README.md
Last updated: YYYY-MM-DD

## Active

## Completed

## Unplanned Specs
- thoughts/shared/specs/01-first-topic.md
- thoughts/shared/specs/02-second-topic.md
- thoughts/shared/specs/03-third-topic.md
```

### 6. Run the loop

```bash
./loop.sh        # default 50 iterations
./loop.sh 20     # max 20 iterations
```

## How It Works

### The State Machine

The loop reads `IMPLEMENTATION_PLAN.md` every iteration to determine mode:

- **PLAN mode** — No active plan with remaining phases
  - Gap analysis → research → create plan
  - Managed by `/ralph_plan`
- **BUILD mode** — Active plan with unchecked phases
  - Implements one phase, verifies, commits
  - Managed by `/ralph_build`

### Signal Protocol

Claude communicates with the loop via signal strings in its final output:

| Signal | Exit Code | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `<done/>` | 0 | All specs satisfied — loop exits |
| Normal exit | 1 | Step complete — continue to next iteration |
| `<blocked reason="..."/>` | 2 | Human intervention needed — loop exits |
| Error | 3 | Claude error — loop continues |

### One-Operation-Per-Invocation

Both plan and build commands do exactly ONE heavy operation per Claude invocation:
- Plan: ONE research session OR ONE plan creation
- Build: ONE phase implementation

This keeps each context window focused and prevents drift.

### The `[DONE]` Protocol

Phase completion is tracked by appending `[DONE]` to phase headings:
- `## Phase 1: Title` → `## Phase 1: Title [DONE]`
- `loop.sh` counts phases without `[DONE]` to determine mode
- `implement_plan.md` writes the marker after verification passes

## Interactive Commands

These can be used standalone (outside the loop) in any Claude Code session:

| Command | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `/create_jtbd` | Create a Jobs to Be Done document |
| `/create_ralph_topics` | Decompose JTBD into behavioral specs |
| `/create_plan` | Create an implementation plan |
| `/iterate_plan` | Update an existing plan |
| `/implement_plan` | Implement a plan phase-by-phase |
| `/research_codebase` | Deep codebase research |
| `/research_codebase_lightweight` | Quick focused research |
| `/commit` | Create git commits |

## Logging

Runtime logs are written to `.ralph/` (gitignored):

- `.ralph/loop.log` — JSONL, one entry per iteration (cost, turns, duration, signals)
- `.ralph/raw/iter_NNN_MODE.jsonl` — Full stream-JSON per iteration

## Prerequisites

- [Claude Code CLI](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code) installed
- Python 3.6+ (for `parse_stream.py`)
- Git repository

## License

MIT

390	
391	## Architecture Documentation
392	
393	**How the existing landing renders**: Server Component (`export default function HomePage()`), imports client components (`HeroChangelogBadge`, `ProductDemo`, etc.) where needed. SEO is JSON-LD structured data (Organization / WebSite / SoftwareApplication / FAQPage) injected via `<JsonLd />` from `@vendor/seo`. Microfrontend `<Link>` from `@vercel/microfrontends/next/client` is used for all internal nav (app is the default microfrontend; `/early-access` is served by the `app` app, not `www`).
394	
395	**How Remotion compositions compose the iso plane**: single CSS 3D context (`rotateX(54.7356deg) rotateZ(-45deg) scale(1.1)`) with `transform-style: preserve-3d`, then all sections are absolutely positioned within a 1536×1536 plane and inherit the isometric transform. Cards use the reusable `IsometricCard` with spring entrance animations. The `logo-animation.tsx` bypasses the CSS 3D plane and uses raw SVG iso projection math for path tracing (Lissajous) — an approach that could be directly reused for static DOM illustrations.
396	
397	**Microfrontend boundary**: `apps/www` (marketing + docs, port 4101) is separate from `apps/app` (auth + app, port 4107). All `/early-access`, `/sign-up`, `/docs/*` paths are served by `apps/app` via `apps/app/microfrontends.json` routing. Landing page lives at the root of `www`.
398	
399	## Historical Context (from thoughts/)
400	
401	No prior research on landing-page restructure was found. The thoughts/ directory contains research on internal architecture (auth flow, webhook handling, entity extraction, proxy system) but nothing on marketing-site design decisions.
402	
403	Relevant memory entry from prior sessions: **Remotion blog featured image rejected designs** (`memory/feedback_remotion_blog_featured_rejected.md`) — 5 rejected concepts (mandalas, grids, scatter) + 5 approved. This likely informs acceptable direction for any new illustrations: avoid mandala/grid/scatter patterns, even isometric ones. The Linear benefits trio are layered planes / card clusters / wave bars — none fall into the rejected categories.
404	
405	## Related Research
406	
407	No directly related prior research in `thoughts/shared/research/`.
408	
409	## Open Questions
410	
411	1. **Palette direction** — Linear uses warm near-black + subtle purple-blue accents; Supabase uses flat dark gray + a single saturated green. Lightfast currently has `--brand-orange` and `--pitch-deck-red` but the current landing page uses neutral-only styling. Does the restructure adopt a single saturated accent (Supabase-style) or stay neutral with subtle brand moments (Linear-style)? — *evaluator: now, quick decision needed before building any illustrations*
412	2. **Isometric angle — 30° (Linear) vs 54.7° (existing Remotion)** — the Remotion tilt is the true isometric mathematical angle; Linear's illustrations appear to use the shallower 30° front-facing convention which reads less "floating" on a marketing page. Reuse Remotion math or adopt Linear's shallower angle? — *evaluator: now*
413	3. **Illustration delivery — static SVG vs rendered Remotion frame vs animated React SVG** — extracted Linear SVGs are animated in DOM via CSS keyframes (dash offset, grid-dot pulse, chevron fade). Ship hand-authored React SVG with CSS animations, or render high-quality PNG stills from Remotion at multiple DPR? — *evaluator: defer; depends on (1)+(2)*
414	4. **Does the single-page rule allow modal deep-dives?** — Supabase's product pillars and Linear's sub-feature tabs (1.1, 1.2, 1.3) open inline detail. A single-page restructure could use shadcn `Dialog`/`Sheet` or in-section `Tabs` without adding routes. Acceptable? — *evaluator: defer; product/UX decision*
415	5. **Testimonials — do we have real quotes yet?** — Supabase uses raw Twitter/X; Linear uses polished 3-card rotator. If no real quotes exist yet, skip the section rather than fabricate. — *evaluator: now; answer is "whatever real material exists"*
416	6. **Module 3 "Act" content** — the v0.1.0 changelog covers REST/SDK/MCP well but "Act" as a pillar implies intent-based resolution across tools which is roadmap, not shipped. Does Module 3 surface only what ships in v0.1.0 (API/SDK/MCP) and defer the "intent resolution" message? — *evaluator: now*
417

**Visual language (high confidence):**
- Background: warm near-black (`#0F0E0D` range, post-March-2026 refresh moved away from cool-blue dark)
- Cards: ~1–2 lightness steps above bg, soft low-opacity warm-gray borders
- Radius: 8–12px cards, rounded tabs, 6px buttons
- Typography: geometric sans for display (48–72px, weight 600–700), 24–32px medium for sub-headlines, 14–16px regular body at ~70% opacity
- **FIG labels**: small uppercase monospace-weight text, loose (no box) at bottom-left/right of illustration, reads like engineering schematic callouts
- Container max-width: ~1200–1280px
- Section padding: ~80–120px vertical; feature modules approach 100vh

**Isometric illustration conventions:**
- Standard 30° isometric (different from Lightfast's 54.7° Remotion tilt — Linear's is shallower)
- Thin strokes (1–1.5px equivalent at display size)
- **Dashed trace line motif** — recurring. No arrowheads; direction implied by animation or grouping
- **Layered stacked-plane motif** — always bottom-left → top-right in iso space
- Subtle drop shadows (soft, not dramatic)
- Accent highlights in purple-to-blue gradient range (brand)
- Ambient loops for in-card illustrations (always running); scroll-triggered reveals for full product UI sections

**Conversion elements:**
- Primary CTA: "Sign up" (nav persistent) + "Get started" (bottom)
- Secondary: "Contact sales", "Open app"
- No pricing on homepage — pricing is a separate route
- Testimonials AFTER features, before bottom CTA
- Social proof bar is a single headline with a specific number

### 5. Supabase landing page structural reference

Research source: `https://supabase.com/`, crawled fresh 2026-04.

**Section order:**
1. **Announcement banner** — one-line bar above nav (e.g. *"State of Startups 2026: Take the survey"*)
2. **Hero** — headline: *"Build in a weekend / Scale to millions"* (two-line display). Sub: *"Supabase is the Postgres development platform. Start your project with a Postgres database, Authentication, instant APIs, Edge Functions, Realtime subscriptions, Storage, and Vector embeddings."* CTAs: "Start your project" (green filled) + "Request a demo" (ghost)
3. **Logo wall** — eyebrow: *"Trusted by fast-growing companies worldwide."* Horizontal strip, no stats
4. **Product pillars (sequential, one per section)** — Postgres Database / Authentication / Edge Functions / Storage / Realtime / Vector / Data APIs. Each pillar: named section with headline, sub-copy, 2–3 feature tags, optional inline code (e.g. `$ supabase functions deploy`). Mix of bento cards and split-layout panels.
5. **Framework logos row** — *"Use Supabase with any framework."*
6. **Customer stories carousel** — three outcome-first headlines rotating (e.g. *"Maergo's Express Delivery: How Supabase Helped Achieve Scalability, Speed, and Cost Saving"*). CTAs: View all stories / View events.
7. **Template gallery** — *"Start building in seconds."* Card grid of community starters.
8. **Tabbed dashboard showcase** — *"Stay productive and manage your app without leaving the dashboard."* Tabs: Table Editor / SQL Editor / RLS Policies.
9. **Community testimonial wall** — *"Join the community."* Masonry/grid of raw Twitter/X post cards (14+), verbatim, with handles. Auto-cycling. CTA: Join us on Discord.
10. **Open-source anchor** — *"Open source from day one."* Copy: *"Read, contribute, self-host. You're never locked in, and always in control."* GitHub badge: `@supabase 100.8K — Top 100 GitHub repos`.
11. **Final CTA** — mirrors hero exactly: *"Build in a weekend, scale to millions."* Same two CTAs.
12. Footer.

**Visual language:**
- Background: `#1C1C1C` (primary) / `#171717` (alt); card `#232323`
- Single brand accent: green `#3ECF8E` — applied sparingly (primary CTA, SQL keywords in code blocks, GitHub star number)
- Text: `#EDEDED` primary / `#A1A1A1` secondary; borders at 6% white default / 12% strong
- Typography: Circular sans (display ~56px at `-0.02em`), Source Code Pro mono (13px at 1.7 line-height for code)
- Radius: 6px buttons, 8px cards, 4px inputs
- Code blocks: 24px padding, `#171717` bg, 1px border at 6% white, syntax highlighting maps green to keywords

**Conversion signals:**
- No pricing numbers on homepage
- Free-tier signal via GitHub stars (100.8K) + "never locked in" copy
- Twitter testimonials (unpolished, with handles) replace enterprise logo wall as primary social proof
- Developer-identity positioning: velocity promise ("weekend"), code as first-class UI, open source as last objection cleared before final CTA

### 6. Proposed section order for the restructured Lightfast landing page

> **⚠ SUPERSEDED (2026-04-15).** The A–M section order below is no longer the active plan. See the Strategic Reset banner at the top of this document for the replacement IA (Hero → Manifesto → Pipeline diagram → Memory → Intelligence → Agents → Bug→Fix→Changelog loop → Built for teams that ship → Changelog/waitlist/footer). The observational research in §1–§5 and §7 remains valid; only this mapping is deprecated.

This is a documentation synthesis mapping existing assets + new isometric sections to a single-page structure. **Not a decided plan** — open questions listed after.

The structure fuses Linear's numbered-module scroll with Supabase's developer-trust anchors, keeping everything on one page.

**A. Nav (already exists)** — `apps/www/src/app/(app)/_components/app-navbar.tsx`

**B. Announcement bar (new, optional)** — thin bar linking to the v0.1.0 changelog. Reuses `HeroChangelogBadge` content but as a full-width dismissible strip.

**C. Hero** (existing, restructure)
- Keep the current headline structure ("Building the superintelligence layer for startups"), consider the Linear-style emphasis split (muted supporting words / bright key nouns already in place at `page.tsx:277-282`)
- Replace the commented-out video hero with a **React-native isometric illustration** based on the Remotion `landing-hero` composition — static SVG version derived from `packages/app-remotion/src/compositions/landing-hero/sections/stream-events.tsx` + `ingested-data.tsx` layered planes
- Keep "Join Early Access" primary CTA, add secondary "Read the docs" or "View on GitHub" per Supabase convention
- Keep `HeroChangelogBadge` at bottom of hero

**D. Benefits trio (NEW — Linear FIG 0.2/0.3/0.4 analogue)**
Tab-carousel with three cards, each captioned `FIG 0.x`, mirroring Linear's structure. Candidate mapping onto Lightfast narrative:
- **FIG 0.2 — "Built for engineering intelligence"** (layered-plane SVG analogous to `01-built-for-purpose.svg`) — positioning card
- **FIG 0.3 — "Powered by a neural pipeline"** (diamond cluster analogous to `02-powered-by-ai-agents.svg`, with dot-grid loaders indicating per-entity extraction) — technical credibility card
- **FIG 0.4 — "Designed for real-time"** (momentum bar wave analogous to `03-designed-for-speed.svg`) — speed/SSE/live-stream card

**E. Integrations row (existing)** — `IntegrationShowcase`. Keep as-is; this is the Supabase logo-wall equivalent.

**F. Module 1 — Observe** (NEW, modelled on Linear's Module 1.0)
- Headline: *"Observe everything that happens"*
- Sub: GitHub, Vercel, Linear, Sentry events flowing in real time via webhooks
- Visual on one side: `ProductDemo` component (already built — renders an event-feed mockup)
- Sub-features: 1.1 Webhook ingestion / 1.2 Entity extraction / 1.3 Real-time SSE / 1.4 Jobs dashboard
- Copy source: `apps/www/src/content/changelog/2026-03-26-initial.mdx:46-54` (the neural-pipeline stages)

**G. Module 2 — Remember** (NEW, modelled on Linear's Module 2.0)
- Headline: *"Memory with sources"*
- Sub: Vector search across extracted entities with cited provenance
- Visual: a new static iso illustration using the **Git Automations hexagon-platter motif** (`04-git-automations-fig-3-6.svg`) — two platters linked by chevron column representing the event → entity → embedding pipeline
- Sub-features: 2.1 Entity graph / 2.2 Vector search / 2.3 Semantic answers / 2.4 Cited sources
- Copy source: changelog MDX lines 59–69

**H. Module 3 — Act** (NEW — product pillar stretch)
- Headline: *"Intent, not API calls"*
- Sub: REST API, TypeScript SDK, MCP server — humans and agents on the same primitives
- Visual: tabbed code block showcase (Supabase pattern) — tabs for CLI / SDK / MCP / REST
- Sub-features: 3.1 Public API / 3.2 TypeScript SDK / 3.3 MCP server / 3.4 API keys
- Copy source: changelog MDX lines 104–127

**I. FAQ (existing)** — `FAQSection`. Keep.

**J. Changelog preview (existing)** — `ChangelogPreview`. Move up to sit adjacent to Module 3 as "Built in public" evidence.

**K. Social proof band (new, thin)** — Twitter/X testimonial wall OR curated quote carousel, depending on available content. Supabase-style masonry if tweets exist; Linear-style 3-card rotator if curated quotes only.

**L. Final CTA (existing)** — `WaitlistCTA`. Keep.

**M. Footer (existing)** — `app-footer`.

This structure has:
- 3 isometric illustrations (Benefits trio) + 2 optional (Module 2 hexagon-platter reuse, optional hero reuse) = 3–5 new illustration surfaces
- Reuses 5 of the 6 existing landing components (`HeroChangelogBadge`, `IntegrationShowcase`, `ProductDemo`, `FAQSection`, `ChangelogPreview`, `WaitlistCTA`)
- Replaces the flat "6 benefits grid" with 3 narrative modules (Observe / Remember / Act), matching the existing pillar vocabulary already used in the codebase

### 7. Design-system primitives available

From `packages/ui/src/components/ui/`:
- Layout: Card, Container, Separator, AspectRatio, ScrollArea, Resizable
- Navigation: Tabs (needed for benefits carousel + Module 3 code tabs), Accordion (already used in FAQ), NavigationMenu
- Surfaces: Button, Badge, Dialog, Sheet, Carousel (available for benefits trio)
- Other: Skeleton, Tooltip, Avatar, Chart

From `packages/ui/src/globals.css:100-127` — custom shifted text scale (text-xs at 0.625rem → text-7xl at 3.75rem) is already in place for tighter hierarchy.

Typography fonts (`apps/www/src/lib/fonts.ts:1-48`):
- PP Neue Montreal (display) — `.font-pp` class applied on headings, line-height 1.1, letter-spacing -0.03em
- Geist Sans / Geist Mono (body / code)

Colour tokens (`packages/ui/src/globals.css:130-203`) — OKLCH-based, full dark-mode variant. Note: Lightfast's palette is near-neutral (no green equivalent to Supabase's `#3ECF8E`); the brand accents are `--brand-orange` (oklch 0.5913 0.2286 31.28) and `--pitch-deck-red`. This differs from both Linear (subtle purple-blue) and Supabase (single green) — the restructure will need a palette decision (see Open Questions).

## Code References

- `apps/www/src/app/(app)/(marketing)/(landing)/page.tsx:23-60` — current benefits array (6 flat cards)
- `apps/www/src/app/(app)/(marketing)/(landing)/page.tsx:223-319` — current hero with commented-out video
- `apps/www/src/app/(app)/(marketing)/(landing)/page.tsx:346-389` — current "Connect Your Tools" benefits grid
- `apps/www/src/content/changelog/2026-03-26-initial.mdx:44-127` — v0.1.0 feature copy ready to surface
- `packages/app-remotion/src/compositions/landing-hero/landing-hero.tsx:21-55` — isometric plane math (tilt 54.7°, rotate-z -45°, plane 1536×1536)
- `packages/app-remotion/src/compositions/landing-hero/shared/grid-lines.tsx:1-157` — dashed iso grid + beam pattern
- `packages/app-remotion/src/compositions/landing-hero/shared/isometric-card.tsx:1-59` — reusable positioned iso card primitive
- `packages/ui/src/globals.css:100-127` — shifted text-size scale
- `packages/ui/src/globals.css:130-203` — OKLCH colour tokens (light + dark)
- `apps/www/src/lib/fonts.ts:1-48` — PP Neue Montreal + Geist font setup
- `thoughts/shared/research/assets/linear-reference/01-built-for-purpose.svg` — extracted SVG for FIG 0.2 motif
- `thoughts/shared/research/assets/linear-reference/02-powered-by-ai-agents.svg` — extracted SVG for FIG 0.3 motif
- `thoughts/shared/research/assets/linear-reference/03-designed-for-speed.svg` — extracted SVG for FIG 0.4 motif
- `thoughts/shared/research/assets/linear-reference/04-git-automations-fig-3-6.svg` — extracted SVG for Git Automations bento

## Architecture Documentation

**How the existing landing renders**: Server Component (`export default function HomePage()`), imports client components (`HeroChangelogBadge`, `ProductDemo`, etc.) where needed. SEO is JSON-LD structured data (Organization / WebSite / SoftwareApplication / FAQPage) injected via `<JsonLd />` from `@vendor/seo`. Microfrontend `<Link>` from `@vercel/microfrontends/next/client` is used for all internal nav (app is the default microfrontend; `/early-access` is served by the `app` app, not `www`).

**How Remotion compositions compose the iso plane**: single CSS 3D context (`rotateX(54.7356deg) rotateZ(-45deg) scale(1.1)`) with `transform-style: preserve-3d`, then all sections are absolutely positioned within a 1536×1536 plane and inherit the isometric transform. Cards use the reusable `IsometricCard` with spring entrance animations. The `logo-animation.tsx` bypasses the CSS 3D plane and uses raw SVG iso projection math for path tracing (Lissajous) — an approach that could be directly reused for static DOM illustrations.

**Microfrontend boundary**: `apps/www` (marketing + docs, port 4101) is separate from `apps/app` (auth + app, port 4107). All `/early-access`, `/sign-up`, `/docs/*` paths are served by `apps/app` via `apps/app/microfrontends.json` routing. Landing page lives at the root of `www`.

## Historical Context (from thoughts/)

No prior research on landing-page restructure was found. The thoughts/ directory contains research on internal architecture (auth flow, webhook handling, entity extraction, proxy system) but nothing on marketing-site design decisions.

Relevant memory entry from prior sessions: **Remotion blog featured image rejected designs** (`memory/feedback_remotion_blog_featured_rejected.md`) — 5 rejected concepts (mandalas, grids, scatter) + 5 approved. This likely informs acceptable direction for any new illustrations: avoid mandala/grid/scatter patterns, even isometric ones. The Linear benefits trio are layered planes / card clusters / wave bars — none fall into the rejected categories.

## Related Research

No directly related prior research in `thoughts/shared/research/`.

## Open Questions

1. **Palette direction** — Linear uses warm near-black + subtle purple-blue accents; Supabase uses flat dark gray + a single saturated green. Lightfast currently has `--brand-orange` and `--pitch-deck-red` but the current landing page uses neutral-only styling. Does the restructure adopt a single saturated accent (Supabase-style) or stay neutral with subtle brand moments (Linear-style)? — *evaluator: now, quick decision needed before building any illustrations*
2. **Isometric angle — 30° (Linear) vs 54.7° (existing Remotion)** — the Remotion tilt is the true isometric mathematical angle; Linear's illustrations appear to use the shallower 30° front-facing convention which reads less "floating" on a marketing page. Reuse Remotion math or adopt Linear's shallower angle? — *evaluator: now*
3. **Illustration delivery — static SVG vs rendered Remotion frame vs animated React SVG** — extracted Linear SVGs are animated in DOM via CSS keyframes (dash offset, grid-dot pulse, chevron fade). Ship hand-authored React SVG with CSS animations, or render high-quality PNG stills from Remotion at multiple DPR? — *evaluator: defer; depends on (1)+(2)*
4. **Does the single-page rule allow modal deep-dives?** — Supabase's product pillars and Linear's sub-feature tabs (1.1, 1.2, 1.3) open inline detail. A single-page restructure could use shadcn `Dialog`/`Sheet` or in-section `Tabs` without adding routes. Acceptable? — *evaluator: defer; product/UX decision*
5. **Testimonials — do we have real quotes yet?** — Supabase uses raw Twitter/X; Linear uses polished 3-card rotator. If no real quotes exist yet, skip the section rather than fabricate. — *evaluator: now; answer is "whatever real material exists"*
6. **Module 3 "Act" content** — the v0.1.0 changelog covers REST/SDK/MCP well but "Act" as a pillar implies intent-based resolution across tools which is roadmap, not shipped. Does Module 3 surface only what ships in v0.1.0 (API/SDK/MCP) and defer the "intent resolution" message? — *evaluator: now*

1	# Specification Language Guide
2	
3	How the specification language should be worded.
4	
5	## 1. Voice and Tense
6	
7	- Present tense, active voice throughout.
8	- Third person only. Never "we" or "you". Use the component name: "the orchestrator decides",
9	  "the service validates", "implementations should document".
10	- Declarative statements for behavior: "The poll loop runs every `interval_ms` milliseconds."
11	- Imperative for requirements: "Normalize labels to lowercase before comparison."
12	
13	## 2. Obligation Keywords
14	
15	| Word     | Meaning                                                        |
16	| -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
17	| must     | Hard requirement. Conformance depends on it.                   |
18	| should   | Strong recommendation. Deviations require justification.       |
19	| may      | Optional. Implementation decides.                              |
20	| required | Field or behavior is mandatory for dispatch/conformance.       |
21	| optional | Field or behavior can be omitted without breaking conformance. |
22	
23	Use "should" as the default for behavioral guidance. Reserve "must" for invariants that, if
24	violated, break correctness or interoperability.
25	
26	## 3. Structural Conventions
27	
28	### Section Headings
29	
30	- `##` for major numbered sections (Problem Statement, Goals, System Overview).
31	- `###` for subsections within a section.
32	- `####` for detailed items (individual entities, config keys, specific behaviors).
33	
34	### Field Definitions
35	
36	Use this format for every field in a domain model or config schema:
37	
38	```
39	- `field_name` (type, constraints)
40	  - Semantic description.
41	  - Default: `value` (if applicable).
42	```
43	
44	Type should be the logical type (`string`, `integer`, `boolean`, `list of strings`, `map`,
45	`timestamp`, `path string`). Include constraints inline: "integer, >= 1", "string or null".
46	
47	### Component Definitions
48	
49	Numbered list. Each entry:
50	
51	```
52	1. `Component Name`
53	   - Verb-led sentence describing what it does.
54	   - Second sentence if needed for scope boundary.
55	```
56	
57	### Goals
58	
59	- Verb-led phrases: "Poll the issue tracker on a fixed cadence."
60	- One goal per bullet.
61	- Specific enough to be testable.
62	
63	### Non-Goals
64	
65	- Gerund or noun phrases: "Rich web UI or multi-tenant control plane."
66	- Clarify scope boundaries, not just negations.
67	- Parenthetical context when useful: "(That logic lives in the workflow prompt.)"
68	
69	## 4. Annotation Patterns
70	
71	Use these labeled blocks to break flow and add context without disrupting the main narrative:
72	
73	- **`Design note:`** — Rationale for a decision. Explains _why_, not _what_.
74	- **`Important boundary:`** — Scope clarification. Draws a line the spec does not cross.
75	- **`Important nuance:`** — Subtle behavioral detail that is easy to miss or misimplement.
76	- **`Note:`** — General supplementary information.
77	- **`Example:`** or **`Example <qualifier>:`** — Illustrative case, not normative.
78	
79	Each annotation starts on its own line as a block, not inline.
80	
81	## 5. State and Lifecycle Descriptions
82	
83	- Name states in backticks with PascalCase: `Running`, `RetryQueued`, `Released`.
84	- Use numbered lists for ordered phase sequences (lifecycle stages).
85	- Use bullet lists for transition triggers (unordered events).
86	- Describe recovery behavior with conditional format:
87	  ```
88	  - <failure class>:
89	    - <recovery action>.
90	    - <continuation behavior>.
91	  ```
92	
93	## 6. Code and Identifiers
94	
95	- Backtick all: field names, state names, config keys, file paths, CLI flags, protocol values.
96	- Use `snake_case` for field and config key names.
97	- Use `PascalCase` for state names, component names, entity type names.
98	- Use `UPPER_SNAKE` for environment variables.
99	- JSON examples use standard formatting with 2-space indent inside fenced blocks.
100	
101	## 7. Tone
102	
103	- Dense, not conversational. Every sentence carries information.
104	- No filler ("it is worth noting that", "in order to").
105	- No hedging ("perhaps", "it might be useful to").
106	- Specificity over abstraction. Prefer "30000 milliseconds" over "a reasonable interval".
107	- When a default exists, state it. When a range is valid, state the bounds.
108

# Specification Language Guide

How the specification language should be worded.

## 1. Voice and Tense

- Present tense, active voice throughout.
- Third person only. Never "we" or "you". Use the component name: "the orchestrator decides",
  "the service validates", "implementations should document".
- Declarative statements for behavior: "The poll loop runs every `interval_ms` milliseconds."
- Imperative for requirements: "Normalize labels to lowercase before comparison."

## 2. Obligation Keywords

| Word     | Meaning                                                        |
| -------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| must     | Hard requirement. Conformance depends on it.                   |
| should   | Strong recommendation. Deviations require justification.       |
| may      | Optional. Implementation decides.                              |
| required | Field or behavior is mandatory for dispatch/conformance.       |
| optional | Field or behavior can be omitted without breaking conformance. |

Use "should" as the default for behavioral guidance. Reserve "must" for invariants that, if
violated, break correctness or interoperability.

## 3. Structural Conventions

### Section Headings

- `##` for major numbered sections (Problem Statement, Goals, System Overview).
- `###` for subsections within a section.
- `####` for detailed items (individual entities, config keys, specific behaviors).

### Field Definitions

Use this format for every field in a domain model or config schema:

```
- `field_name` (type, constraints)
  - Semantic description.
  - Default: `value` (if applicable).
```

Type should be the logical type (`string`, `integer`, `boolean`, `list of strings`, `map`,
`timestamp`, `path string`). Include constraints inline: "integer, >= 1", "string or null".

### Component Definitions

Numbered list. Each entry:

```
1. `Component Name`
   - Verb-led sentence describing what it does.
   - Second sentence if needed for scope boundary.
```

### Goals

- Verb-led phrases: "Poll the issue tracker on a fixed cadence."
- One goal per bullet.
- Specific enough to be testable.

### Non-Goals

- Gerund or noun phrases: "Rich web UI or multi-tenant control plane."
- Clarify scope boundaries, not just negations.
- Parenthetical context when useful: "(That logic lives in the workflow prompt.)"

## 4. Annotation Patterns

Use these labeled blocks to break flow and add context without disrupting the main narrative:

- **`Design note:`** — Rationale for a decision. Explains _why_, not _what_.
- **`Important boundary:`** — Scope clarification. Draws a line the spec does not cross.
- **`Important nuance:`** — Subtle behavioral detail that is easy to miss or misimplement.
- **`Note:`** — General supplementary information.
- **`Example:`** or **`Example <qualifier>:`** — Illustrative case, not normative.

Each annotation starts on its own line as a block, not inline.

## 5. State and Lifecycle Descriptions

- Name states in backticks with PascalCase: `Running`, `RetryQueued`, `Released`.
- Use numbered lists for ordered phase sequences (lifecycle stages).
- Use bullet lists for transition triggers (unordered events).
- Describe recovery behavior with conditional format:
  ```
  - <failure class>:
    - <recovery action>.
    - <continuation behavior>.
  ```

## 6. Code and Identifiers

- Backtick all: field names, state names, config keys, file paths, CLI flags, protocol values.
- Use `snake_case` for field and config key names.
- Use `PascalCase` for state names, component names, entity type names.
- Use `UPPER_SNAKE` for environment variables.
- JSON examples use standard formatting with 2-space indent inside fenced blocks.

## 7. Tone

- Dense, not conversational. Every sentence carries information.
- No filler ("it is worth noting that", "in order to").
- No hedging ("perhaps", "it might be useful to").
- Specificity over abstraction. Prefer "30000 milliseconds" over "a reasonable interval".
- When a default exists, state it. When a range is valid, state the bounds.

1	# Reaper Service Specification
2	
3	Status: Draft v1 (language-agnostic)
4	
5	Purpose: Define a service that monitors deployments and rolls back failed releases automatically.
6	
7	## 1. Problem Statement
8	
9	Reaper is a long-running automation service that watches deployment pipelines, detects failures
10	using health checks, and triggers automatic rollbacks when thresholds are breached.
11	
12	The service solves three operational problems:
13	
14	- It replaces manual rollback decisions with configurable automated health evaluation.
15	- It isolates rollback execution per deployment so concurrent releases do not interfere.
16	- It keeps rollback policy in-repo (`ROLLBACK.md`) so teams version their thresholds and
17	  recovery steps with their code.
18	
19	Important boundary:
20	
21	- Reaper is a monitor and rollback executor.
22	- Deployment triggering and promotion logic live outside this service.
23	- A successful rollback ends at a policy-defined stable state, not necessarily the prior version.
24	
25	## 2. Goals and Non-Goals
26	
27	### 2.1 Goals
28	
29	- Poll deployment status on a fixed cadence with bounded concurrency.
30	- Maintain a single authoritative state for active monitors, rollback decisions, and cooldowns.
31	- Evaluate health checks against configurable thresholds before triggering rollback.
32	- Support multiple deployment targets with per-target rollback policies.
33	- Recover from transient health-check failures with exponential backoff.
34	- Load rollback behavior from a repository-owned `ROLLBACK.md` contract.
35	- Expose operator-visible observability (at minimum structured logs).
36	
37	### 2.2 Non-Goals
38	
39	- Deployment orchestration or promotion pipelines.
40	- Rich web UI or multi-tenant control plane.
41	- General-purpose monitoring or alerting system.
42	- Built-in business logic for notification routing. (That logic lives in the rollback policy
43	  and external integrations.)
44	
45	## 3. System Overview
46	
47	### 3.1 Main Components
48	
49	1. `Policy Loader`
50	   - Reads `ROLLBACK.md`.
51	   - Parses YAML front matter and policy body.
52	   - Returns `{config, policy_template}`.
53	
54	2. `Config Layer`
55	   - Exposes typed getters for policy config values.
56	   - Applies defaults and environment variable indirection.
57	
58	3. `Deployment Client`
59	   - Fetches active deployments from the deployment platform.
60	   - Fetches health metrics for specific deployments.
61	   - Normalizes platform payloads into a stable deployment model.
62	
63	4. `Monitor`
64	   - Owns the poll tick.
65	   - Owns the in-memory runtime state.
66	   - Decides which deployments to watch, evaluate, rollback, or release.
67	
68	5. `Rollback Executor`
69	   - Builds rollback command from deployment context and policy template.
70	   - Executes rollback as a subprocess.
71	   - Reports outcome back to the monitor.
72	
73	6. `Logging`
74	   - Emits structured runtime logs to one or more configured sinks.
75	
76	### 3.2 External Dependencies
77	
78	- Deployment platform API (for example Kubernetes, Vercel, or custom).
79	- Health-check endpoint or metrics source.
80	- Rollback tooling accessible from the host environment.
81	- Host environment authentication for the deployment platform.
82	
83	## 4. Core Domain Model
84	
85	### 4.1 Entities

file.content:
# Reaper Service Specification

Status: Draft v1 (language-agnostic)

Purpose: Define a service that monitors deployments and rolls back failed releases automatically.

## 1. Problem Statement

Reaper is a long-running automation service that watches deployment pipelines, detects failures
using health checks, and triggers automatic rollbacks when thresholds are breached.

The service solves three operational problems:

- It replaces manual rollback decisions with configurable automated health evaluation.
- It isolates rollback execution per deployment so concurrent releases do not interfere.
- It keeps rollback policy in-repo (`ROLLBACK.md`) so teams version their thresholds and
  recovery steps with their code.

Important boundary:

- Reaper is a monitor and rollback executor.
- Deployment triggering and promotion logic live outside this service.
- A successful rollback ends at a policy-defined stable state, not necessarily the prior version.

## 2. Goals and Non-Goals

### 2.1 Goals

- Poll deployment status on a fixed cadence with bounded concurrency.
- Maintain a single authoritative state for active monitors, rollback decisions, and cooldowns.
- Evaluate health checks against configurable thresholds before triggering rollback.
- Support multiple deployment targets with per-target rollback policies.
- Recover from transient health-check failures with exponential backoff.
- Load rollback behavior from a repository-owned `ROLLBACK.md` contract.
- Expose operator-visible observability (at minimum structured logs).

### 2.2 Non-Goals

- Deployment orchestration or promotion pipelines.
- Rich web UI or multi-tenant control plane.
- General-purpose monitoring or alerting system.
- Built-in business logic for notification routing. (That logic lives in the rollback policy
  and external integrations.)

## 3. System Overview

### 3.1 Main Components

1. `Policy Loader`
   - Reads `ROLLBACK.md`.
   - Parses YAML front matter and policy body.
   - Returns `{config, policy_template}`.

2. `Config Layer`
   - Exposes typed getters for policy config values.
   - Applies defaults and environment variable indirection.

3. `Deployment Client`
   - Fetches active deployments from the deployment platform.
   - Fetches health metrics for specific deployments.
   - Normalizes platform payloads into a stable deployment model.

4. `Monitor`
   - Owns the poll tick.
   - Owns the in-memory runtime state.
   - Decides which deployments to watch, evaluate, rollback, or release.

5. `Rollback Executor`
   - Builds rollback command from deployment context and policy template.
   - Executes rollback as a subprocess.
   - Reports outcome back to the monitor.

6. `Logging`
   - Emits structured runtime logs to one or more configured sinks.

### 3.2 External Dependencies

- Deployment platform API (for example Kubernetes, Vercel, or custom).
- Health-check endpoint or metrics source.
- Rollback tooling accessible from the host environment.
- Host environment authentication for the deployment platform.

## 4. Core Domain Model

### 4.1 Entities

#### 4.1.1 Deployment

Normalized deployment record used by monitoring and rollback.

Fields:

- `id` (string)
  - Stable platform-internal ID.
- `name` (string)
  - Human-readable deployment identifier.
- `status` (string)
  - Current platform status.
- `health_score` (float, 0.0-1.0, or null)
  - Latest evaluated health metric.
- `created_at` (timestamp or null)

#### 4.1.2 Rollback Policy

Parsed `ROLLBACK.md` payload:

- `config` (map)
  - YAML front matter root object.
- `policy_template` (string)
  - Markdown body after front matter, trimmed.

I've read the 2026-04-15 strategic reset, both harness SPEC templates (`SPEC_core.md` structure + `SPEC_language.md` voice rules), current `README.md`, and current `SPEC.md`. Before diving in, a read + a branching point:

## Where each artifact stands

- **README.md** — already aligned with the reset (Memory → Intelligence → Agents pipeline, no Linear/GitHub-first framing, v0.1.0 scope is honest). Mostly fine; room to tighten the "Loop" section and tune the pitch line.
- **SPEC.md** — already follows the harness `SPEC_core.md` skeleton (Mission, Vision, Problem, Goals/Non-Goals, Abstraction Layers, Main Components, Entities with `shipped/partial/planned` tags). Voice is largely compliant with `SPEC_language.md`. Cleanup is refinement, not rewrite.
- **Landing page** — the 2026-04-15 reset locked the IA (Hero → Manifesto → Pipeline diagram → Memory → Intelligence → Agents → Bug→Fix→Changelog loop → Built for teams → Changelog/waitlist). What's missing is a **fresh observational pass** tied to the locked IA, not the superseded §6 A–M.

## Proposed split

1. **Landing-page design** — fresh agent-browser pass on Linear (`/`, `/build`, `/plan`) + Supabase (`/`, `/features`, a product pillar page) with the locked IA in mind. Output: a new research doc that maps Linear/Supabase patterns onto the eight sections of the reset IA, plus component-level proposals.
2. **SPEC.md refinement** — pass against `SPEC_language.md` checklist (voice, obligation keywords, field format) without expanding implementation detail.
3. **README.md tune-up** — tighten against the locked positioning.

## Before I start — three questions

1. **agent-browser invocation** — you flagged a preference for agent-browser, no MCP wrappers. Do you want me to shell out to `agent-browser` via Bash (what's the CLI invocation?), or do you run it and paste output into the thread?
2. **Scope order** — landing-page research first (biggest blocker, most novel), then SPEC.md/README.md polish? Or all three in parallel?
3. **Pages to target** — Linear `/`, `/build`, `/plan` and Supabase `/`, `/features`, one pillar page? Anything else worth hitting (Vercel, Clerk, Resend)?

content.text:
# Research Codebase

You are tasked with conducting comprehensive research across the codebase to answer user questions by spawning parallel sub-agents and synthesizing their findings.

## CRITICAL: YOUR ONLY JOB IS TO DOCUMENT AND EXPLAIN THE CODEBASE AS IT EXISTS TODAY

- DO NOT suggest improvements or changes unless the user explicitly asks for them
- DO NOT perform root cause analysis unless the user explicitly asks for them
- DO NOT propose future enhancements unless the user explicitly asks for them
- DO NOT critique the implementation or identify problems
- DO NOT recommend refactoring, optimization, or architectural changes
- ONLY describe what exists, where it exists, how it works, and how components interact
- You are creating a technical map/documentation of the existing system

## Initial Setup:

When this command is invoked, respond with:

```
I'm ready to research the codebase. Please provide your research question or area of interest, and I'll analyze it thoroughly by exploring relevant components and connections.
```

Then wait for the user's research query.

## Steps to follow after receiving the research query:

1. **Read any directly mentioned files first:**
   - If the user mentions specific files (tickets, docs, JSON), read them FULLY first
   - **IMPORTANT**: Use the Read tool WITHOUT limit/offset parameters to read entire files
   - **CRITICAL**: Read these files yourself in the main context before spawning any sub-tasks
   - This ensures you have full context before decomposing the research

2. **Analyze and decompose the research question:**
   - Break down the user's query into composable research areas
   - Take time to ultrathink about the underlying patterns, connections, and architectural implications the user might be seeking
   - Identify specific components, patterns, or concepts to investigate
   - Create a research plan using TodoWrite to track all subtasks
   - Consider which directories, files, or architectural patterns are relevant

3. **Spawn parallel sub-agent tasks for comprehensive research:**
   - Create multiple Task agents to research different aspects concurrently
   - We now have specialized agents that know how to do specific research tasks:

   **For codebase research:**
   - Use the **codebase-locator** agent to find WHERE files and components live
   - Use the **codebase-analyzer** agent to understand HOW specific code works (without critiquing it)
   - Use the **codebase-pattern-finder** agent to find examples of existing patterns (without evaluating them)

   **IMPORTANT**: All agents are documentarians, not critics. They will describe what exists without suggesting improvements or identifying issues.

   **For thoughts directory:**
   - Use the **thoughts-locator** agent to discover what documents exist about the topic
   - Use the **thoughts-analyzer** agent to extract key insights from specific documents (only the most relevant ones)

   **For web research (only if user explicitly asks):**
   - Use the **web-search-researcher** agent for external documentation and resources
   - IF you use web-research agents, instruct them to return LINKS with their findings, and please INCLUDE those links in your final report

   The key is to use these agents intelligently:
   - Start with locator agents to find what exists
   - Then use analyzer agents on the most promising findings to document how they work
   - Run multiple agents in parallel when they're searching for different things
   - Each agent knows its job - just tell it what you're looking for
   - Don't write detailed prompts about HOW to search - the agents already know
   - Remind agents they are documenting, not evaluating or improving

4. **Wait for all sub-agents to complete and synthesize findings:**
   - IMPORTANT: Wait for ALL sub-agent tasks to complete before proceeding
   - Compile all sub-agent results (both codebase and thoughts findings)
   - Prioritize live codebase findings as primary source of truth
   - Use thoughts/ findings as supplementary historical context
   - Connect findings across different components
   - Include specific file paths and line numbers for reference
   - Highlight patterns, connections, and architectural decisions
   - Answer the user's specific questions with concrete evidence

5. **Gather metadata for the research document:**
   - Get the current git commit hash: `git rev-parse HEAD`
   - Get the current branch: `git branch --show-current`
   - Get today's date
   - Filename: `thoughts/shared/research/YYYY-MM-DD-description.md`
     - Format: `YYYY-MM-DD-description.md` where:
       - YYYY-MM-DD is today's date
       - description is a brief kebab-case description of the research topic

6. **Generate research document:**
   - Use the metadata gathered in step 5
   - Structure the document with YAML frontmatter followed by content:

     ```markdown
     ---
     date: [Current date and time with timezone in ISO format]
     researcher: claude
     git_commit: [Current commit hash]
     branch: [Current branch name]
     topic: "[User's Question/Topic]"
     tags: [research, codebase, relevant-component-names]
     status: complete
     last_updated: [Current date in YYYY-MM-DD format]
     ---

     # Research: [User's Question/Topic]

     **Date**: [Current date and time with timezone]
     **Git Commit**: [Current commit hash]
     **Branch**: [Current branch name]

     ## Research Question

     [Original user query]

     ## Summary

     [High-level documentation of what was found, answering the user's question by describing what exists]

     ## Detailed Findings

     ### [Component/Area 1]

     - Description of what exists (`file.ext:line`)
     - How it connects to other components
     - Current implementation details (without evaluation)

     ### [Component/Area 2]

     ...

     ## Code References

     - `path/to/file.ts:123` - Description of what's there
     - `another/file.ts:45-67` - Description of the code block

     ## Architecture Documentation

     [Current patterns, conventions, and design implementations found in the codebase]

     ## Historical Context (from thoughts/)

     [Relevant insights from thoughts/ directory with references]

     - `thoughts/shared/something.md` - Historical decision about X

     ## Related Research

     [Links to other research documents in thoughts/shared/research/]

     ## Open Questions

     [Any areas that need further investigation]
     ```

7. **Add GitHub permalinks (if applicable):**
   - Check if on main branch or if commit is pushed: `git branch --show-current` and `git status`
   - If on main/master or pushed, generate GitHub permalinks:
     - Get repo info: `gh repo view --json owner,name`
     - Create permalinks: `https://github.com/{owner}/{repo}/blob/{commit}/{file}#L{line}`
   - Replace local file references with permalinks in the document

8. **Evaluate open questions (automatic):**
   - If the research document has an Open Questions section with items, this step runs automatically
   - Spawn the **open-questions-evaluator** agent with:
     - The path to the research document you just wrote
     - The path to `SPEC.md`
   - The evaluator will triage each question as **"now"**, **"defer"**, or **"already answered"**

   **For "now" questions:**
   - Use `AskUserQuestion` to surface the quick-answerable questions to the user:

     ```
     The research produced these open questions that can be answered quickly:
     1. [Question] — [evaluator's reason]
     2. [Question] — [evaluator's reason]

     Which would you like me to answer now? (all / numbers / none)
     ```

   - For each question the user approves, run `/research_codebase_lightweight` with:
     - The specific question
     - The parent research document path
     - The SPEC.md path
   - Run approved questions in parallel when possible
   - Each produces a separate followup research file linked from the parent

   **For "defer" questions:**
   - Leave them in the Open Questions section as-is
   - Append a note: `(deferred — needs dedicated research session)`

   **For "already answered" questions:**
   - Remove them from Open Questions and add the reference the evaluator found

   **If no open questions exist**, skip this step entirely.

9. **Present findings:**
   - Present a concise summary of findings to the user
   - Include key file references for easy navigation
   - Note any followup research files that were generated
   - Ask if they have follow-up questions or need clarification

10. **Handle follow-up questions:**
    - If the user has follow-up questions, append to the same research document
    - Update the frontmatter field `last_updated` to reflect the update
    - Add `last_updated_note: "Added follow-up research for [brief description]"` to frontmatter
    - Add a new section: `## Follow-up Research [timestamp]`
    - Spawn new sub-agents as needed for additional investigation
    - Continue updating the document

## Important notes:

- Always use parallel Task agents to maximize efficiency and minimize context usage
- Always run fresh codebase research - never rely solely on existing research documents
- The thoughts/ directory provides historical context to supplement live findings
- Focus on finding concrete file paths and line numbers for developer reference
- Research documents should be self-contained with all necessary context
- Each sub-agent prompt should be specific and focused on read-only documentation operations
- Document cross-component connections and how systems interact
- Include temporal context (when the research was conducted)
- Link to GitHub when possible for permanent references
- Keep the main agent focused on synthesis, not deep file reading
- Have sub-agents document examples and usage patterns as they exist
- Explore all of thoughts/ directory, not just research subdirectory
- **CRITICAL**: You and all sub-agents are documentarians, not evaluators
- **REMEMBER**: Document what IS, not what SHOULD BE
- **NO RECOMMENDATIONS**: Only describe the current state of the codebase
- **File reading**: Always read mentioned files FULLY (no limit/offset) before spawning sub-tasks
- **Critical ordering**: Follow the numbered steps exactly
  - ALWAYS read mentioned files first before spawning sub-tasks (step 1)
  - ALWAYS wait for all sub-agents to complete before synthesizing (step 4)
  - ALWAYS gather metadata before writing the document (step 5 before step 6)
  - NEVER write the research document with placeholder values
- **Frontmatter consistency**:
  - Always include frontmatter at the beginning of research documents
  - Keep frontmatter fields consistent across all research documents
  - Update frontmatter when adding follow-up research
  - Use snake_case for multi-word field names (e.g., `last_updated`, `git_commit`)
  - Tags should be relevant to the research topic and components studied
